learning_outcomes.description: Interpret human activity at a range of scales, from individual sites to broader processes such as colonialism and capitalism
Apply key historical archaeological theory and method to research problems, drawing on diverse sources of evidence
Describe the political, ethical and professional frameworks that shape how historical archaeology is practised
Identify and record the characteristics for common classes of artefacts found in Australian historical archaeological contexts

assessments.task_description:
For this assessment, you will complete a sequence of three in-class quizzes. Quizzes will occur at the beginning of the timetabled lecture in Weeks 4, 8 and 13.

The quizzes will each contain multiple choice or short answer questions. Quizzes are designed to be completed in 20 minutes and you will have 30 minutes to complete each one.

Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Each quiz will be in-person, within the timetabled lecture.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
For this assignment you will record data for 12 historical burial memorials from one cemetery and prepare a report that thematically discusses the results. The list of eligible cemeteries is:

  1. Alberton
  2. Bald Hills
  3. Balmoral
  4. Beenleigh
  5. Bethania
  6. Brookfield
  7. Caboolture
  8. Cleveland
  9. Dunwich
  10. Eagleby
  11. Ipswich
  12. Logan Village
  13. Nudgee Catholic
  14. Nundah
  15. South Brisbane (Dutton Park)
  16. Southport
  17. Toowong

To complete this assessment, you will:

  1. Select a cemetery from the above list.
  2. Visit the cemetery (take means to record the memorials, a camera and a tape measure).
  3. Identify pre-WWI (pre-1914) burial memorials in the cemetery. These may be in one area or across the cemetery.
  4. Select twelve memorials for this study that have a range of variables suitable for identifying a pattern.
  5. Record the details of each memorial. You can use the recording form provided on Blackboard, or create your own form (such as excel spreadsheet or notebook). You should fill in every section of the form or include the same information in another format.
  6. Take at least one photo of each memorial and at least one photo of yourself at the cemetery. Please be respectful while you are recording and photographing memorials.
  7. Prepare a concise report that includes:

A.    A summary of the data collected for the 12 burial memorials.

B.    Presentation of one pattern you observed across the memorials, relating to variables such as age, sex, year of death or the grave materials.

C.    Discussion of this pattern in relation to a theme and relevant historical archaeological theory.

D.    An appendix of your record form/s and photographs of each memorial.

The word limit for the report is 2500 words, including tables and in-text references, but not including appendices and your reference list. This is a maximum word count (not +/- 10%); if you can meet the report requirements more concisely, it is acceptable to submit a report with a shorter word limit.

8. Submit your completed report via the Turnitin submission portal.

Additional guiding information about selecting and recording memorials, and preparing the report will be supplied in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard and discussed during lectures (Weeks 3 and 4).

Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students may be required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assessment item must be submitted via Turnitin on Blackboard Ultra.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
15/10/2026

This task will be scheduled to occur at a specified time within the timetabled Exam.
assessments.task_description:
This is an in-class examination of the skills you have acquired in the practical workshops on the various material classes of historical artefacts.

Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assessment requires students to attend in-person for an allocated 60-minute exam within the timetabled Exam for Week 11.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
What is historical archaeology?

An introduction to historical and contemporary archaeology and where they sit within the broader discipline. (Course and assessment overview is provided separately on Blackboard.)

Learning outcomes: L01, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: What is historical archaeology? An introduction to historical and contemporary archaeology and where they sit within the broader discipline. (Course and assessment overview is provided separately on Blackboard.)

learning_activities.topic:
Evidence in historical archaeology

What counts as evidence for historical archaeologists, and how this shapes the methods we use.

Learning outcomes: L02, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Evidence in historical archaeology What counts as evidence for historical archaeologists, and how this shapes the methods we use.

learning_activities.topic:
Working with artefacts

Get hands-on with the core methods historical archaeologists use to turn artefacts into interpretation. This foundational practical builds the handling, recording and analytical skills you'll draw on in every practical that follows.

Learning outcomes: L02, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Working with artefacts Get hands-on with the core methods historical archaeologists use to turn artefacts into interpretation. This foundational practical builds the handling, recording and analytical skills you'll draw on in every practical that follows.

learning_activities.topic:
Themes and theory in historical archaeology

A tour through key theoretical lenses and themes historical archaeologists use to interpret the archaeological record.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Themes and theory in historical archaeology A tour through key theoretical lenses and themes historical archaeologists use to interpret the archaeological record.

learning_activities.topic:
Cemetery studies

Why and how historical archaeologists study cemeteries, with a look at the Old Sydney Burial Ground, isotopic analysis, and what doing this kind of work looked like in 1970s Australia! Sets up the cemetery assignment.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Cemetery studies Why and how historical archaeologists study cemeteries, with a look at the Old Sydney Burial Ground, isotopic analysis, and what doing this kind of work looked like in 1970s Australia! Sets up the cemetery assignment.

learning_activities.topic:
Ceramic artefacts

Learn to identify, characterise and date historical ceramics. Through hands-on exercises with 19th- and 20th-century material, you'll practise the work-related skills used by professional historical archaeologists and heritage consultants when analysing ceramic assemblages.

Learning outcomes: L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Ceramic artefacts Learn to identify, characterise and date historical ceramics. Through hands-on exercises with 19th- and 20th-century material, you'll practise the work-related skills used by professional historical archaeologists and heritage consultants when analysing ceramic assemblages.

learning_activities.topic:
Interconnected networks

Using the archaeological record to trace capitalism and global trade, explored through the successive waves of colonisation on Norfolk Island.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Interconnected networks Using the archaeological record to trace capitalism and global trade, explored through the successive waves of colonisation on Norfolk Island.

learning_activities.topic:
Colonialism and its entanglements

How colonial archaeology reveals the entangled relationships between peoples, using case studies from the Cape York Peninsula.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Colonialism and its entanglements How colonial archaeology reveals the entangled relationships between peoples, using case studies from the Cape York Peninsula.

learning_activities.topic:
Glass artefacts

An introduction to the historical archaeological analysis of glass, including identification, manufacturing techniques and dating methods. Through practical exercises with 19th- and 20th-century glass artefacts, you'll build the work-related skills used to interpret glass assemblages from historical sites.

Learning outcomes: L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Glass artefacts An introduction to the historical archaeological analysis of glass, including identification, manufacturing techniques and dating methods. Through practical exercises with 19th- and 20th-century glass artefacts, you'll build the work-related skills used to interpret glass assemblages from historical sites.

learning_activities.topic:
Cultural complexity in the historical period

Reading social stratification, gender, ethnicity and age through material culture, with a focus on Point Puer, lutruwita/Tasmania, a prison for convict children.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Cultural complexity in the historical period Reading social stratification, gender, ethnicity and age through material culture, with a focus on Point Puer, lutruwita/Tasmania, a prison for convict children.

learning_activities.topic:
Power

How the archaeological record reveals inequality and power relations, explored through plantation landscapes.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Power How the archaeological record reveals inequality and power relations, explored through plantation landscapes.

learning_activities.topic:
Metals, plastics and building materials

Identification, manufacture and dating of metal, plastic and building material artefacts. Hands-on exercises let you practise the work-related skills needed to interpret 19th- and 20th-century objects in these material categories.

Learning outcomes: L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Metals, plastics and building materials Identification, manufacture and dating of metal, plastic and building material artefacts. Hands-on exercises let you practise the work-related skills needed to interpret 19th- and 20th-century objects in these material categories.

learning_activities.topic:
Stratigraphy and analysis

Historical archaeology often involves highly stratified sites and complex stratigraphic relationships. This week we'll look more closely at how analysis in historical archaeology employs method and theory for stratigraphy-based analysis.

Learning outcomes: L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Stratigraphy and analysis Historical archaeology often involves highly stratified sites and complex stratigraphic relationships. This week we'll look more closely at how analysis in historical archaeology employs method and theory for stratigraphy-based analysis.

learning_activities.topic: Mid Semester Break
learning_activities.topic_description: Mid Semester Break

learning_activities.topic:
Institutions and ideology

What institutions are, why they emerged in the modern world, and how archaeology captures both institutional agendas and lived experience — using a site that shifted from convict lumber yard to asylum.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Institutions and ideology What institutions are, why they emerged in the modern world, and how archaeology captures both institutional agendas and lived experience — using a site that shifted from convict lumber yard to asylum.

learning_activities.topic:
Consolidating your practical skills

This week we will consolidate our knowledge of historical artefacts with some structured exercises, as well as spending time preparing for the practical exam.

Learning outcomes: L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Consolidating your practical skills This week we will consolidate our knowledge of historical artefacts with some structured exercises, as well as spending time preparing for the practical exam.

learning_activities.topic:
Hidden worlds

Illicit behaviour and the social forces shaping it, through a secret cache of artefacts found at the Royal Derwent Hospital, lutruwita/Tasmania.. 

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Hidden worlds Illicit behaviour and the social forces shaping it, through a secret cache of artefacts found at the Royal Derwent Hospital, lutruwita/Tasmania..

learning_activities.topic:
In-class artefact practical exam (Assessment)

The exam will occur in a 1-hour timeslot within the timetabled class.

Learning outcomes: L04
learning_activities.topic_description: In-class artefact practical exam (Assessment) The exam will occur in a 1-hour timeslot within the timetabled class.

learning_activities.topic:
Urban historical archaeology

Archaeology in and of the city, using Melbourne's 'Little Lon', the idea of the 'slum', and artefacts recovered from cesspits.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Urban historical archaeology Archaeology in and of the city, using Melbourne's 'Little Lon', the idea of the 'slum', and artefacts recovered from cesspits.

learning_activities.topic:
Archaeology in the present

What an archaeology of/for the present looks like, via the Transit Van Project and a recent Australian vehicle archaeology study, and a return to historical archaeology's relevance today.

Learning outcomes: L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Archaeology in the present What an archaeology of/for the present looks like, via the Transit Van Project and a recent Australian vehicle archaeology study, and a return to historical archaeology's relevance today.
